one_line_takeaway: A black-box, prompt-perturbation interpretability method (UNPACT) shows current LLM unlearning methods work by suppressing the model's attention to a few keyword tokens rather than erasing knowledge, so simply re-emphasizing those keywords in the prompt recovers roughly twice as much 'forgotten' content as prior sampling-based probes, revealing an unavoidable trade-off between recoverable (insufficient) and destructive (catastrophic-forgetting) unlearning.
key_finding: Table 1: the proportion of cases with 'correct focus' (KEYTOKENS overlap pre/post-unlearning) drops when knowledge is forgotten vs. retained, most sharply on News (average decrease of 34.2% across the six methods, e.g. RMU 66.7%->56.9% (-9.8) vs. GAGD 56.2%->31.9% (-24.3) vs. TV 72.0%->27.3% (-44.7)), moderately on Books (average -21.4%), and only slightly on WMDP (average -6.3%, attributed to the constrained multiple-choice response format). Table 2: FOCUSONKEY recovery rates average 51.2% (News), 30.7% (Books), and 54.4% (WMDP) across the six unlearning methods, versus the PROBAB sampling baseline's 33.8% (News), 15.3% (Books), and 31.3% (WMDP) -- i.e., roughly 2x higher recovery than PROBAB while using only greedy decoding (e.g., on WMDP: NPOGD PROBAB 9.4% -> FOCUSONKEY 48.6%, a +39.2-point jump; on News: NPOKL PROBAB 39.1% -> FOCUSONKEY 69.2%, +30.5 points). Qualitative case studies (Figs. 3-5) show KEYTOKENS shift away from correct-answer-supporting tokens when knowledge is forgotten but remain stable when retained, and that catastrophic-forgetting outputs (e.g., degenerate repeated-token strings) arise when the model loses focus on essentially all prompt tokens rather than just target-knowledge tokens. Figure 6 visualizes, for each of the six methods across checkpoints, a recovery-rate vs. destructive-rate frontier showing all methods leave a persistent gap to the 'reliable unlearning' origin (e.g., RMU is described as resistant to catastrophic forgetting but unable to further reduce recovery rate; NPOKL moves from high recoverability toward high destructiveness as training proceeds).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is UNPACT (UNlearning via Prompt Attribution and ConTribution tracking), a black-box, weight-free interpretability method that quantifies each input prompt token's causal contribution to an LLM's generated answer by measuring the drop in log-probability of the output when a token is masked/removed from the prompt (C(x_i,y) = LP(x,y) - LP(x\{x_i},y)), then extracts the top-contributing tokens as KEYTOKENS via a normalized-threshold/small-set rule (thresholds alpha, beta tuned by grid search). Using UNPACT to compare pre- and post-unlearning models across six unlearning methods, three LLMs, and three benchmarks, the authors derive three original empirical findings: (1) unlearning works largely by disrupting the model's attention to KEYTOKENS rather than erasing knowledge; (2) explicitly re-emphasizing the pre-unlearning KEYTOKENS in the prompt (their proposed recovery technique, FOCUSONKEY) restores much of the 'forgotten' knowledge without touching model weights; (3) catastrophic forgetting results from unlearning losses penalizing all prompt tokens indiscriminately, including common/functional words unrelated to the target knowledge. They also propose a joint 'recovery rate' / 'destructive rate' framework to visualize the resulting dilemma across unlearning methods and checkpoints.
threat_model: Not an adversarial attacker-vs-defender threat model in the classic sense; the paper treats unlearning itself as a safety/privacy mechanism (used, per WMDP, to suppress hazardous biosecurity/cybersecurity/chemistry knowledge, and per News/Books to remove copyrighted or sensitive content) and probes its robustness under a realistic constraint: the adversary/tester has only black-box, input/output access to the post-unlearning model (no weight edits, no fine-tuning, no internal activations), can only modify the prompt, and is restricted to greedy decoding. Under this constrained black-box setting they show that simply appending a short instruction emphasizing identified keywords (e.g., 'Focus on [keywords] to answer.') is enough to resurface knowledge the unlearning method was supposed to have deleted, which is directly relevant to the security question of whether unlearning-as-a-guardrail (e.g., against WMDP hazardous knowledge) can be bypassed by ordinary prompt engineering rather than requiring extraction attacks on model internals.
limitations: The authors' own framing already surfaces the central limitation as their thesis (an unavoidable dilemma between insufficient forgetting and catastrophic forgetting), but concrete methodological limitations visible in the text: (1) hyperparameters for each unlearning method were selected to be 'representative and reasonably optimized' per recommendations in prior work (Shi et al. 2024; Li et al. 2024c) rather than exhaustively tuned, which the paper itself flags could affect generalizability of specific numbers; (2) KEYTOKENS selection depends on two tunable thresholds (alpha, beta) chosen via grid search, introducing a hyperparameter-sensitivity axis not deeply stress-tested in the excerpted sections; (3) FOCUSONKEY's improved recovery on smaller (7B/8B/14B) models was found in preliminary experiments to require also including question tokens (not just knowledge KEYTOKENS) to avoid quality degradation -- a model-scale-dependent caveat noted only briefly (deferred to Appendix E); (4) results are reported only for three specific LLMs (Llama-2-7B, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Qwen3-14B) and three text domains (News, Harry Potter Books, WMDP-biosecurity), leaving open whether findings generalize to larger models or other unlearning targets (e.g., WMDP cybersecurity/chemistry subsets are not evaluated, only biosecurity); (5) evaluation relies on GPT-4o-mini as an LLM judge rather than human evaluation, introducing potential judge-model bias/error not independently validated in the excerpted portion.

datasets_used: News: BBC news articles collected after August 2023 (Li et al., 2023)
Books: Harry Potter book series (Eldan & Russinovich, 2023)
WMDP: multiple-choice biosecurity-domain questions from the WMDP benchmark (Li et al., 2024c)
quantitative_results: Table 1 (correct-focus proportion, %): News Retained avg 69.6% -> Forgotten avg 35.4% (avg drop 34.2%); Books Retained avg 49.6% -> Forgotten avg 28.2% (avg drop 21.4%); WMDP Retained avg 24.3% -> Forgotten avg 18.0% (avg drop 6.3%)
Table 2 (recovery rate, %, FOCUSONKEY vs PROBAB, average across 6 unlearning methods): News 51.2% vs 33.8% (+17.4 pts); Books 30.7% vs 15.3% (+15.4 pts); WMDP 54.4% vs 31.3% (+23.1 pts)
Largest single-method jumps: NPOGD on WMDP 9.4%->48.6% (+39.2); NPOKL on News 39.1%->69.2% (+30.5); GAGD on News 12.8%->27.7% (+14.9)
baselines_compared: PROBAB: probabilistic recovery baseline via multinomial sampling (Scholten et al., 2024), compared against FOCUSONKEY's greedy-decoding recovery
ROUGE-L automatic metric (Lin, 2004), explicitly rejected in favor of GPT-4o-mini LLM-as-judge for answer correctness evaluation

Six unlearning methods evaluated as the object of study, not as competing baselines to UNPACT: GA (Jang et al., 2022), NPO (Zhang et al., 2024b), Task Vector/TV (Ilharco et al., 2022), RMU (Li et al., 2024c), plus GA/NPO combined with GD and KL regularizers (GAGD, GAKL, NPOGD, NPOKL)
